overview: The public library service for Clarendon began in 1951 when a small library was established at the back of the stands of the Muir Park Pavilion in May Pen, the chief town or capital. This small building erected at the cost of six hundred dollars ($600.00), was a fine example of community initiative and effort. The Parish Library Committee planned and constructed the building and many residents contributed gifts of materials and cash. There were one thousand three hundred and thirty (1,330) books and six hundred and twenty (620) readers.

Set against the blue- green Caribbean the Hanover Parish Library in Lucea offers a constant invitation to young and old to access information in its spacious rooms. The building was erected in two stages, the first wing in 1963 and the second in 1965. Built at a total cost of thirty-five thousand dollars ($35,000), it was designed by the firm of Harold Ashwell and Associates who pioneered the design of public library buildings in Jamaica.Notably, the main feature of the Ashwell-designed public library was the level which provided maximum ventilation and light without reducing the wall area available for shelving. The books were also protected from direct sunlight by this positioning of the windows.

parish_page_title: Kingston & St. Andrew Parish Library Network
overview: The Kingston and St. Andrew Parish Library on Tom Redcam Drive bears the pen name of Thomas Henry, MacDermot, Jamaica’s first poet laureate. The library is centrally located near Cross Roads where the major traffic routes of the city converge. The service to the city began in 1955 when a rented house at Brentford Road was attractively remodelled to accommodate a library and fifteen thousand (15,000) books were provided. The service was so well received that membership had to be closed within the first month of the opening date. Over eighty-nine thousand (89,000) books were loaned in eight (8) months. The library was housed for three (3) years at that location.

The movement of Free Public Libraries began in 1937 by the late Walter L. Lewis, Presbyterian Minister, who circulated a pamphlet called A Plea for Manchester ‘Free’ library in which he stated: “One of the greatest needs of Jamaica is ‘Free’ libraries.” On July 6, 1937, a public meeting was held at the Roof Garden Theatre in Mandeville and a committee was formed to promote the establishment of the library. In the following year, on August 1, the Manchester Free Library was officially opened by His Excellency, Sir Edward Denham.

Portland can be regarded as one of the pioneer parishes in library development. As early as 1824 the St. Georges Library Society began in Portland as a subscription library in Buff Bay. It was an active and progressive library whose members had to pay both an admission fee and an annual subscription fee. In 1838 there were 79 members. This library set the pattern for the development of similar subscription libraries in St. Elizabeth, St. James, St. Ann, Trelawny and St. Catherine. Nearly 100 years later the idea of free libraries was able to gain favourable public support in most of these parishes.

Nora Bateson championed a vision of libraries as agents of personal and community development. In 1945 her “library plan for Jamaica” noted that a group in St. Ann’s Bay had been discussing the prospects for the free library for the parish and were in correspondence with the Institute of Jamaica on the matter. This group was led by Mr. and Mrs. G. Louis Byles formally launched the idea of St. Ann Public Libraries at a public meeting held in St. Ann’s Bay on March 13, 1947. A number of gifts were formally presented from Croydon and Aylesbury Libraries in England, from the British Council and residents of New York and citizens of Jamaica. A committee of 16 persons with Mr. Byles as chairman was formed to organize the collection of funds and to set up the library.

The St. Catherine Parish Library had its early beginning in July 1947, when Spanish Town Citizen Association met, with Mr. Hugh Paget, then British Council Representative in Jamaica, and discussed the formation of a library in Spanish Town. Central Government was approached for accommodation and two of the rooms now occupied by the library were made available for the purpose. A small committee was in charge who worked towards raising funds and obtaining furniture for the library. Central Government and then Parochial Board each contributed One hundred ($100) dollars and many citizens, public offices, and leading business firms gave donations of books, cash, and furniture.

The St. Elizabeth Parish Library was founded by the late Rev. Walter Lewis, a Presbyterian minister and pioneer in public library development in Jamaica, who saw the need for a library in the thriving logwood town of Black River. A committee of interested persons was set up under the chairmanship of Mrs. Julia Johnston and three months later, on February 5, 1944, the Library became a reality. It began operations in a room above a store in down town Black River, when it was officially opened as the St. Elizabeth Public Library.

The public library service in St. James was established in 1948 (public library - 1944 - 1948) by the late Mrs. R. Leyson Ewen (then Mrs. Joan Crum- Ewing) and a small group of members of the Young Women’s Christian Association (Y.W.C.A.) led by Dr. Sylvia Lowe. The ladies made the decision to collect second-hand books with which to start a library for the public of Montego Bay. A donation of one hundred and fifty (150) books was given to the library by the Y. W. C. A, and within a short period a stock of five hundred (500) books was collected and repaired by the group. The Child Welfare Association made a room available, rent free in its building at 43 Church Street and the Y.W.C.A. again assisted with the loan of furniture. The opening of the St. James Parish Library took place on June 1, 1944, and Y.W.C.A. members served as voluntary librarians. The library remained at the Child Welfare Association building for nine (9) years. The founders of the library played a pivotal role in the library’s development.

St. Mary was among the last of the parishes to establish a public library service. Although there was a small library service started in Highgate as early as 1947, this was organized by a community group. It was not until 1951 the Parochial Board provided in its estimates the necessary funds to operate a library service in the parish. The statutory committee appointed under the Jamaica Library Service (JLS) law held its inaugural meeting in September, 950. Mr. A. H. Val Cottrell, then Clerk of the courts for St. Mary, was elected chairman and the committee set about making arrangements for establishing a library in Port Maria. Mr. Cottrell and late Mr. H. B. Phillpotts another member of the committee undertook the task of finding accommodation for the library.

St. Thomas Parish Library owes the first 21 years of its development to the initiative of the late Miss Inez C. Bowen who in 1947 garnered support from leading citizens of Morant Bay for the formation of a public library for that town. A resolution to this effect was passed in 1948 and Miss Bowen met with the committee to outline her proposal. In response, Mr. Douglas Moyston, committee member and solicitor of the firm Moyston and Silvera offered temporary accommodation for the library at the firm’s offices.

The Trelawny Parish Library in Falmouth was first located in an early nineteenth-century structure at the corner of Market Street near the Court House. From 1949 to 1961 both the adult and children’s libraries occupied the coach house a building which originally housed the Northside Club, a social meeting -place for the residents of Falmouth during the nineteenth century. In 1961 the two sections of the library were separated and the children’s library was transferred to the first floor of the main building. However, from the library could be seen the Court House, an impressive building in the Georgian style which was built in 1815 and remodeled after the fire of 1826.

The Rev. Canon Henry Williams Cope, started the Westmoreland Library. He was the Rector of the St. George’s Anglican Church in Savanna-la-mar. The library at that time was built up mainly from donations and was housed in the Anglican Church Hall. This small service was operated by Miss Hazel Drummond who served as first voluntary Librarian from 1946 to 1948. A number of persons worked assiduously towards the development of the library and served as members of the managing committee under the chairmanship of the Rev. Canon Cope. By May 1948 when the Jamaica Library Service was established, there were seven hundred (700) books and seventy (70) adult members and this service became the nucleus for the formation of the Westmoreland Parish library .
